title: Whittled hazel pot stirrer
subtitle: A round-section kitchen stirrer from a green hazel rod
excerpt: A round-section pot stirrer whittled from a green hazel rod is a one-session project that requires nothing beyond a sloyd knife and a strop. The green wood cuts easily, the form is simple, and the finished stirrer needs no hollowing. A good first project for someone who has never whittled before.
sourceNotes: Domestic spoon and stirrer forms from green-wood rods documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in 19th-century rural craft texts.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently harvested wood, still high in moisture content. Cuts much more readily than seasoned timber under a knife.
A carving cut in which the blade travels away from the body, driven by forearm extension. The default cut for working a round-section rod.
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound used to maintain a sharp edge between honings.

projectSchedule.title: Whittle the stirrer in one session
projectSchedule.body: The green hazel shapes quickly. Allow one to two hours.

projectSchedule.title: Sand and oil once the stirrer has dried
projectSchedule.body: Leave the carved stirrer to dry for a week before sanding and oiling. Drying slightly firms the surface.

projectSchedule.title: Oil cured: food-safe
projectSchedule.body: Two weeks after oiling, the raw linseed oil has cured. The stirrer is ready for kitchen use.

content.text: A pot stirrer from a green hazel rod is the simplest starting project in wood carving. The rod arrives as a cylinder; the work is to taper one end toward a flat paddle, round the other end into a comfortable handle, and remove the bark along the whole length.
Green wood
cuts with noticeably less resistance than seasoned timber, which makes this an ideal first project for developing feel for the
push cut
before moving to harder or more complex work.
What you need: wood

A straight green hazel rod, 30 to 35 cm long and 2 to 2.5 cm in diameter. Hazel grows as a coppice shrub across most of Britain and western Europe and produces straight rods of consistent diameter, which makes it ideal for this project. A coppice-cut rod of one to two years' growth from the stool is the right size. If hazel is not accessible, ash or willow of the same dimensions work equally well. Willow cuts slightly more easily than hazel but dries to a more variable surface.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife with a sharp edge. A
strop
. Sandpaper in 180 and 240 grit for after the stirrer has dried. Raw linseed oil. No other tools are needed; this project is intentionally single-tool.
Workspace

A garden chair, a low stool, or any stable outdoor seat. Green-wood whittling outdoors avoids the wood chips and shavings indoors; they are easier to sweep from grass or stone than from carpet or upholstery. Good natural light. No other preparation needed.
The work
Step 1: Remove the bark

Hold the rod in the four-finger grip, thumb on top, and work push cuts along the length to peel the bark. Green hazel bark peels very easily; a firm push cut at a shallow angle takes it off in long strips. Remove all bark from the full length of the rod. The freshly peeled surface will be pale green-white and smooth. Work with the grain: push cuts upward from the butt (thick end) toward the tip peel cleanly; cuts in the opposite direction on some sections of a young hazel rod may tear. Adjust direction at any point where the surface feels rough.
Step 2: Taper the paddle end

The working end of the stirrer (the bottom 10 cm) is thinned to a flat paddle shape. Flatten two opposite faces of the rod in this section by taking push cuts along the length, removing material from one face and then the other in even passes. The finished thickness at the very tip is around 8 mm; it should not be too thin or it will flex awkwardly in use. The sides of the paddle stay round-sectioned rather than square-edged; a few light push cuts along the side corners will round them.
Step 3: Refine the handle

The handle occupies the top 12 to 15 cm of the rod and stays close to the original round section. Round off any natural nodes (the small swellings at branch junction points) with push cuts. Check the handle in the hand: it should feel smooth with no abrupt ridges or hard spots. The transition between handle and paddle should be a gradual taper, not a sharp step.
Step 4: Dry before sanding

Leave the carved stirrer to dry for a week in a warm, ventilated place. Green hazel dries quickly in a warm room; a week is usually enough for a rod this diameter. As it dries, the surface will develop small raised grain fibres (this is called grain raise and is normal in green wood that has been carved and then dried). These are removed by the sanding step.
Step 5: Sand

After drying, sand through 180 and 240 grit along the length of the rod. The raised grain fibres from drying come away quickly on 180 grit. Work along the grain, not across it. Wipe away dust between grits.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil, allow to penetrate for one hour, wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact. After two weeks the stirrer is fully food-safe.
Variations

Make the paddle end pointed rather than flat for a spurtle-style stirrer: thin it to a rounded point on all four sides rather than flattening it. A pointed end reaches into the corners of a saucepan more effectively.
Leave the bark on the handle section and remove it only from the paddle. Hazel bark is smooth and pleasant to hold; the bark-on handle has a rustic appearance that some makers prefer.
Left-handed variant: a pot stirrer is symmetrical; no adjustment needed.
Care
Hand wash and dry immediately. Do not soak. Re-oil once or twice a year. A stirrer in daily use will darken gradually with cooking fats and oils absorbed into the wood, which is normal.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that come up whittling a green hazel rod.

items.symptom: The rod splits along its length as it dries
items.cause: The stirrer dried too quickly, or the walls are uneven in places (one face thicker than the other), causing differential shrinkage.
items.fix: For future projects, coat the end-grain ends with PVA or wax after carving to slow end-grain drying. Keep the carved stirrer out of direct sun and hot radiators during the first drying week.

items.symptom: The knife tears the wood surface rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: The cut is going against the grain, or through a knot or node in the rod.
items.fix: Try the cut in the opposite direction. At nodes and knots, take very light cuts from several directions toward the high point rather than one heavy cut through it.

items.symptom: The paddle end is twisted slightly rather than flat
items.cause: Material was removed from the faces in unequal passes, or the original rod had a slight curve that was built into the paddle section.
items.fix: Sight along the paddle end in good light. Identify the high side and take a few corrective push cuts on that face. A slight twist in the finished stirrer is functionally acceptable.
